text: The surgeon observes a grossly distended gallbladder with a green discoloration, indicating potential gangrenous changes.
The surgeon prepares to decompress the distended gallbladder, which exhibits a green appearance, to facilitate easier handling during the procedure.
The surgeon elevates the gallbladder, which appears grossly distended with a green hue, indicating possible gangrenous changes.
The surgeon begins blunt dissection of the gallbladder, which appears grossly distended with a green hue and gangrenous changes.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a retractor to manage the grossly distended gallbladder, which has a green appearance.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the gallbladder, which is positioned above its base, indicating the dissection is occurring higher up on the gallbladder structure.
The surgeon performs dissection high up on the gallbladder wall, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection on the gallbladder, manipulating it by retracting medially and laterally to achieve the critical view of the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects downward along the presumed hepatocystic triangle, continuing the dissection from a higher position on the gallbladder wall.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum along the undersurface, initially believed to be the cystic duct, and continues dissection up the lateral right side of the gallbladder.
The gallbladder appears edematous with a friable wall, indicating swelling and delicate tissue that may be prone to tearing.
The surgeon observes gangrenous changes in the gallbladder wall, indicating areas of necrosis and discoloration.
The surgeon realizes the dissection is occurring high up on the gallbladder, indicating that the bottom of the gallbladder has not yet been reached.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the bottom of the gallbladder to visualize the junction of the cystic duct and the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon continues to work on dissecting the bottom of the gallbladder, aiming to expose the junction of the cystic duct and the hepatocystic triangle, with visible gangrenous changes and edema in the gallbladder wall.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum at the bottom of the gallbladder to better elevate and visualize the junction of the cystic duct and hepatocystic triangle.
The surgeon places the junction of the cystic duct under tension while working at the bottom of the gallbladder, aiming to better visualize the hepatocystic triangle.
The junction of the cystic duct and the hepatocystic triangle begins to come into view as the surgeon continues to elevate and dissect the bottom of the gallbladder.
The video shows the initial peritoneal incision made higher than intended, highlighting the incorrect location of the incision.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the surgical field, ensuring visibility and maintaining the proper anatomical plane during the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to clear the hepatocystic triangle, focusing on removing surrounding tissue to expose the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery within the surgical field, visible amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visualizes the junction of the cystic duct with the gallbladder, revealing the anatomical connection between these structures.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to further clear the hepatocystic triangle, enhancing visibility of the junction between the cystic duct and gallbladder.
The surgeon elevates the bottom of the gallbladder to achieve the critical view, enhancing the exposure of the hepatocystic triangle for further dissection.
The surgeon provides a posterior view of the cystic duct, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery adjacent to the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to develop the window around the cystic duct, enhancing the visibility of the hepatocystic triangle.
Lymphovascular tissue is observed obstructing the surgeon's ability to retract the gallbladder, requiring careful manipulation to clear the view.
The surgeon prepares to take the cystic duct, which is visible posteriorly, while maintaining elevation of the gallbladder to achieve the critical view.
The surgeon cleans the posterior aspect of the cystic duct, removing surrounding tissue to improve visibility and access.
The surgeon creates a window in the hepatocystic triangle, carefully maneuvering around the cystic duct to expose the area.
The surgeon addresses a minor arterial bleed caused during manipulation, deciding to ligate the artery to control the bleeding.
The surgeon refrains from taking the cystic duct, focusing on achieving a clearer critical view within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins working on creating a window in the hepatocystic triangle, focusing on careful dissection around the area.
The surgeon continues blunt dissection, staying within the proper anatomical plane to facilitate separation of tissue layers, which visibly fall apart with minimal resistance.
The surgeon identifies lymphovascular tissue still attaching the gallbladder, indicating further dissection is needed to fully mobilize it.
The surgeon thins out the tissue around ductal or vessel structures, continuing the dissection to achieve better exposure of these anatomical elements.
The surgeon carefully thins out the lymphovascular tissue holding the gallbladder using blunt dissection, ensuring the proper plane is maintained before proceeding with further steps.
The surgeon achieves a more complete critical view of the gallbladder, ensuring it is safe to proceed with taking the duct.
The surgeon applies clips to secure the duct, ensuring it is safely occluded before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon divides the thinned ductal or vessel structures after careful dissection.
The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the liver bed, completing the dissection and separation of the gallbladder from surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon identifies posterior vessels adjacent to the gallbladder and carefully separates them to avoid causing bleeding.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver edge, occasionally entering the liver tissue while progressing towards the top of the surgical field.
The gallbladder remains partially attached to the top of the liver, serving as a retractor while the surgeon prepares to completely separate it.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis in the liver bed and irrigates the area, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The surgeon amputates the gallbladder from the liver, using it as a retractor during the procedure.
